summary: Scott Walker's attacks on the University of Wisconsin misidentify the real crisis in higher education: the predatory for-profit college industry. These schools have exploded in the past decade, targeting vulnerable populations with fraudulent marketing while extracting billions in federal aid. Students leave with crushing debt, poor education, and low graduation rates. Rather than Obama's proposed ranking system, O'Neil argues for aggressive fraud detection and redirecting public funds from for-profits to community colleges and state universities that actually serve the public good.
key_points: For-profit colleges game federal student aid programs, with Corinthian alone obtaining $1.4 billion in federal dollars in 2010—more than all 10 UC campuses combined
The industry deliberately targets vulnerable populations (poor, minority, single mothers) with misleading ads and aggressive 'poke the pain' recruitment tactics
Community colleges can't meet demand partly because public funds are being diverted to for-profits, forcing students into more expensive predatory alternatives
Even when fraud is discovered, neither students nor taxpayers get their money back
Education as a profit center transforms admissions counselors into salespeople and students into consumers to be exploited
money_quotes: When education becomes a profit center, things go awry: admissions counselors become salespeople, students become consumers to be wrung for every last dime, and administrators become executives who cash out while students and taxpayers are left with the tab.
They represent a revenue-maximizing industry which game the federal student aid programs for the poor and for veterans.
The industry specifically targets vulnerable, poor, minority single mothers online with misleading ads promising an easy degree and a new life.
Once they have a phone number, they have trained recruiters repeatedly call and 'poke the pain' of their targets.
People who have been told to get a degree to pull themselves out of poverty are often faced with two options: enrolling at a nearby community college, or at a for-profit.
Instead he should create a flat-out fraud detection system, built explicitly to be harder to game than the current watered-down regulatory framework.
key_insight: The real higher education crisis isn't lazy professors at state schools but a predatory for-profit industry that extracts billions in public funds while targeting vulnerable populations with fraudulent promises.
